title: The price of checking just once
summary: Reflect on the hidden cost of 'just checking' a phone once, and argue whether the real problem is the time lost or the attention that never fully returns, using present perfect, conditionals, and opinion phrases.
topic: Is the true cost of a quick phone check the lost minute, or the broken attention?
prompt: Write a 3-paragraph reflective essay (400–500 words) about the experience of 'just checking' a phone for one second in the middle of a task. Argue whether the real cost is the small amount of time you lose, or the way your attention takes much longer to come back to where it was. Use a concrete personal example. Use the present perfect, a first conditional, and at least one opinion phrase such as 'In my view' or 'It seems to me'.
hintStarters: I used to believe that glancing at my phone for one second cost me nothing, until…
It seems to me that the minute I lose is not really the problem, because…
If I leave my phone in another room while I work, then…

hintVocab.phrase: to pick up where you left off
hintVocab.meaningVi: tiếp tục từ chỗ bạn đã dừng
cái giá của sự xao nhãng
tự làm gián đoạn chính mình
thiết lập lại sự chú ý của bạn
một thói quen bạn hầu như không nhận ra

sampleText:
I used to believe that glancing at my phone for one second cost me nothing. A second is nothing, after all. But last winter I tried an experiment that changed my mind. I was writing a difficult report, and every time I reached for my phone, I wrote down the exact time, and then the time when I was truly back inside the report again — not just looking at the screen, but thinking properly. The gap was never one second. It was usually six or seven minutes. I have repeated this little test many times since then, and the result has been frustratingly consistent. The glance is cheap. The recovery is expensive.

It seems to me that the lost minute is not really the problem. If the cost were only the time, I could simply work a few minutes longer and break even. The real damage is harder to measure. When I check my phone in the middle of a sentence, I do not return to that sentence. I return to a blurry idea of where I was, and I have to rebuild it. My train of thought has not paused politely, waiting for me; it has scattered, and I have to gather it again from the beginning. In my view, this is why an afternoon full of small interruptions can feel exhausting even though I have produced very little. The tiredness does not come from the work. It comes from starting the same work twenty times.

I have not solved this, and I am suspicious of people who claim they have. But I have found one rule that helps more than any app. If I leave my phone in another room while I work, the second of checking becomes a whole decision: I have to stand up, walk, and come back. That small wall of effort is usually enough to keep me in my chair and inside my thought. The phone is not evil, and I am not trying to escape the century I live in. What I am trying to protect is the rare, quiet state in which one idea leads to the next without a gap. That state has become harder to reach, and I have come to think it is worth defending the way I would defend sleep or a long meal with friends. The glance feels free because the bill arrives later, quietly, in minutes I never notice losing. I would rather pay attention to where my attention goes.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Second conditional to discuss an unreal situation
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'If the cost were only the time, I could simply work a few minutes longer' dùng điều kiện loại hai ('were'… 'could'…) để bàn về một tình huống không có thật — trên thực tế cái giá không chỉ là thời gian.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'If the cost were only the time, I could simply work a few minutes longer' uses the second conditional ('were' … 'could' …) to discuss an unreal situation — in reality the cost is not only the time.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: First conditional for a real, repeatable rule
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'If I leave my phone in another room while I work, the second of checking becomes a whole decision' dùng điều kiện loại một (hiện tại đơn + hiện tại đơn) để mô tả một quy tắc thật, có thể lặp lại với kết quả gần như chắc chắn.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'If I leave my phone in another room while I work, the second of checking becomes a whole decision' uses the first conditional (present + present) to describe a real, repeatable rule with an almost certain result.

sampleTranslationVi:
Tôi từng tin rằng liếc điện thoại một giây không làm tôi mất gì. Một giây thì có là gì đâu. Nhưng mùa đông năm ngoái tôi đã thử một thí nghiệm làm tôi thay đổi suy nghĩ. Tôi đang viết một bản báo cáo khó, và mỗi lần tôi với lấy điện thoại, tôi ghi lại thời điểm chính xác, rồi cả thời điểm khi tôi thực sự quay trở lại bên trong bản báo cáo — không chỉ nhìn vào màn hình, mà suy nghĩ một cách thực sự. Khoảng cách không bao giờ là một giây. Nó thường là sáu hoặc bảy phút. Tôi đã lặp lại bài kiểm tra nhỏ này nhiều lần kể từ đó, và kết quả luôn nhất quán một cách bực bội. Cái liếc thì rẻ. Việc phục hồi thì đắt.

Đối với tôi, phút bị mất không thực sự là vấn đề. Nếu cái giá chỉ là thời gian, tôi chỉ cần làm thêm vài phút và hòa vốn. Thiệt hại thực sự khó đo lường hơn. Khi tôi kiểm tra điện thoại giữa một câu, tôi không quay lại câu đó. Tôi quay lại với một ý niệm mờ nhạt về nơi mình từng ở, và tôi phải dựng lại nó. Mạch suy nghĩ của tôi đã không dừng lại một cách lịch sự, chờ đợi tôi; nó đã tan rã, và tôi phải gom lại từ đầu. Theo tôi, đây là lý do tại sao một buổi chiều đầy những gián đoạn nhỏ có thể cảm thấy kiệt sức ngay cả khi tôi làm được rất ít. Sự mệt mỏi không đến từ công việc. Nó đến từ việc bắt đầu cùng một công việc hai mươi lần.

Tôi chưa giải quyết được điều này, và tôi nghi ngờ những người tuyên bố họ đã làm được. Nhưng tôi đã tìm thấy một quy tắc giúp ích hơn bất kỳ ứng dụng nào. Nếu tôi để điện thoại ở một phòng khác khi tôi làm việc, giây kiểm tra đó trở thành cả một quyết định: tôi phải đứng dậy, đi bộ, và quay lại. Bức tường nỗ lực nhỏ đó thường là đủ để giữ tôi trên ghế và bên trong suy nghĩ của mình. Điện thoại không phải là điều xấu xa, và tôi không cố trốn khỏi thế kỷ mình đang sống. Điều tôi đang cố bảo vệ là trạng thái yên tĩnh hiếm hoi trong đó ý này dẫn đến ý kế tiếp mà không có khoảng trống. Trạng thái đó đã trở nên khó đạt tới hơn, và tôi đã đi đến chỗ nghĩ rằng nó đáng được bảo vệ như cách tôi sẽ bảo vệ giấc ngủ hay một bữa ăn dài với bạn bè. Cái liếc cảm thấy miễn phí vì hóa đơn đến muộn hơn, lặng lẽ, trong những phút tôi không bao giờ nhận ra mình đang mất. Tôi thà chú ý đến nơi sự chú ý của mình đi đến.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the present perfect correctly for a repeated action up to now?
options: I repeated this little test many times since then.
I have repeat this little test many times since then.
I have repeated this little test many times since then.
I am repeating this little test many times since then.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Have repeated … since then' is the present perfect for actions from a past point up to now. Option B uses simple past with 'since', which is wrong. Option C uses the base 'repeat' instead of the participle. Option D uses the present continuous, which does not fit 'since then'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Since then' pairs with the present perfect, not the simple past.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the second conditional correctly for an unreal situation?
options: If the cost was only the time, I will simply work longer.
If the cost is only the time, I could simply work longer.
If the cost were only the time, I can simply work longer.
If the cost were only the time, I could simply work longer.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Were … could' is the standard second conditional for an unreal present. Option B mixes 'was' with 'will'. Option C uses present 'is', making it a first conditional. Option D uses 'can', which weakens the hypothetical sense after 'were'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the first conditional correctly for a real, repeatable rule?
options: If I leave my phone in another room, the check becomes a whole decision.
If I will leave my phone in another room, the check becomes a whole decision.
If I leave my phone in another room, the check became a whole decision.
If I left my phone in another room, the check becomes a whole decision.
mcQuestions.explanation: First conditional uses present in both clauses for a real rule. Option B puts 'will' in the if-clause. Option C uses past 'became' as the result. Option D mixes past 'left' with present 'becomes'.
mcQuestions.hint: Do not put 'will' in the if-clause of a first conditional.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option is the most natural opinion phrase to introduce a personal view?
options: It seems at me that the lost minute is not the problem.
It seems to me that the lost minute is not the problem.
It seems for me that the lost minute is not the problem.
It seems by me that the lost minute is not the problem.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'It seems to me that …' is the fixed opinion phrase. Options B, C, and D use the wrong prepositions 'at', 'for', and 'by'.
mcQuestions.hint: The fixed phrase takes the preposition 'to'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence most naturally describes returning to a vague memory of your task?
options: I return at a blurry idea of where I was.
I return to a blurry idea of where was I.
I return to a blurry idea of where I was.
I return to a blurry idea of where I were.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Return to' plus normal word order 'where I was' is correct. Option B uses 'at'. Option C inverts to 'where was I' as if it were a direct question. Option D uses 'were' with the singular 'I'.
mcQuestions.hint: After 'where', keep statement word order: subject then verb.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'come to think' correctly to show a changed view?
options: I have came to think it is worth defending.
I have come to thinking it is worth defending.
I have come think it is worth defending.
I have come to think it is worth defending.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Have come to think' uses the participle 'come' and 'to + base verb'. Option B uses 'came' after 'have'. Option C uses 'thinking' after 'to'. Option D drops 'to'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Come to + base verb' means 'gradually start to'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence most naturally expresses the idea that the tiredness comes from repeated restarts?
options: It comes from starting the same work twenty times.
It comes from to start the same work twenty times.
It comes of starting the same work twenty times.
It comes from start the same work twenty times.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Come from + gerund' is the correct pattern. Option B uses 'to start' after 'from'. Option C uses 'of' instead of 'from'. Option D uses the base verb 'start' after 'from'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'From' is a preposition, so it takes the '-ing' form.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'would rather' to state a preference?
options: I would rather to pay attention to where my attention goes.
I would rather pay attention to where my attention goes.
I would rather paying attention to where my attention goes.
I would rather pays attention to where my attention goes.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Would rather + base verb' is correct. Option B adds 'to'. Option C uses 'paying'. Option D uses 'pays'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Would rather' is followed by the bare infinitive.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence most naturally describes a thought that has not waited for you?
options: My train of thought has not pause politely.
My train of thought have not paused politely.
My train of thought has not paused politely.
My train of thought has not paused polite.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Has not paused' is correct present perfect, and 'politely' is the adverb modifying the verb. Option B uses base 'pause'. Option C uses 'have' with a singular subject. Option D uses the adjective 'polite' instead of the adverb.
mcQuestions.hint: Singular subject takes 'has', and you need an adverb to modify the verb.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence best contrasts the cheap glance with the costly recovery?
options: The glance is cheap. The recovery is expensively.
The glance is cheaply. The recovery is expensive.
The glance is cheap. The recovery are expensive.
The glance is cheap. The recovery is expensive.
mcQuestions.explanation: Both clauses use adjectives after 'is' with singular agreement. Option B uses the adverb 'expensively'. Option C uses the adverb 'cheaply'. Option D uses 'are' with the singular 'recovery'.
mcQuestions.hint: After 'is', use an adjective, and match the singular subject.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence most naturally says the cost is hidden because it arrives later?
options: The bill arrives later, in minutes I never notice losing.
The bill arrives later, in minutes I never notice to lose.
The bill arrives later, in minutes I never notice losing it.
The bill arrives lately, in minutes I never notice losing.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Notice + gerund (losing)' is correct, and 'later' is the right adverb of time. Option B uses 'to lose' after 'notice'. Option C adds a redundant 'it'. Option D uses 'lately' (meaning 'recently'), which changes the meaning.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Notice' takes a gerund here, and 'later' (not 'lately') means 'at a later time'.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The writer argues that the real cost of a quick phone check is not the lost minute but the broken attention that takes minutes to rebuild. Do you agree that attention is a resource worth protecting like sleep or rest? What would you be willing to give up to keep your concentration whole?
